note_data.title: Further equilibria
keyPoints: **Ka** is the acid dissociation constant showing the extent of acid ionisation in aqueous solution
A **buffer solution** maintains approximately constant pH when small amounts of acid or base are added
Buffer action occurs because the weak acid removes added OH⁻ ions and the conjugate base removes added H⁺ ions
**Ksp** is the solubility product constant for sparingly soluble ionic compounds at equilibrium
Common ion effect decreases solubility when an ion common to the equilibrium is added
**Partition coefficient** shows how a solute distributes between two immiscible solvents at equilibrium
pH calculations use Ka values and the expression Ka = [H⁺][A⁻]/[HA]
Buffer pH calculations use the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA])
Precipitation occurs when the ionic product exceeds the solubility product Ksp

definitions.definition: The equilibrium constant for the dissociation of a weak acid in aqueous solution
A solution that maintains approximately constant pH when small amounts of acid or base are added
The equilibrium constant for a sparingly soluble ionic solid in equilibrium with its ions in saturated solution
The ratio of concentrations of a solute in two immiscible solvents at equilibrium at constant temperature
The decrease in solubility of an ionic compound when a solution already contains one of the ions from that compound

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the definition or value without explanation
Give the reason using equilibrium principles and Le Chatelier's principle
Show all working with correct units and significant figures
Use given data to work out the answer with reasoning shown

modelAnswer.question: A buffer solution contains 0.10 mol dm⁻³ ethanoic acid and 0.15 mol dm⁻³ sodium ethanoate. Calculate the pH of this buffer. (Ka for ethanoic acid = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ mol dm⁻³)
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct use of Henderson-Hasselbalch equation

commonMistakes: Never confuse Ka with Kb - Ka is for acids, Kb is for bases
Students often forget to convert pKa to Ka or vice versa using the relationship pKa = -log Ka
Don't use total volume in Ksp calculations - use molar concentrations only
Never assume [H⁺] = [acid] for weak acids - must use Ka expression
Students often forget that buffer capacity decreases as the ratio [A⁻]/[HA] moves away from 1:1
examinerTips: This topic appears in every Paper 2 with calculations worth 6-8 marks
Always show working for pH calculations step by step
Check your answer is reasonable - strong acids have pH < 2, weak acids pH 3-6
For Ksp questions, write the equilibrium expression first before substituting values
Buffer questions often link to titration curves - know the connection
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Buffer action diagram showing weak acid HA and conjugate base A⁻ removing added H⁺ and OH⁻ ions respectively, with equilibrium arrows and the resulting pH stability
